author: mariusvanderwijden
content:
I might not be able to join the call today, so I want to propose an alternative to 8254.
8254 in its current specification forces clients to keep a block wide counter for deposits for an edge case.
My counterproposal would be to only ever return the first 512 deposits to the CL.
Every deposit that goes beyond the limit would just be ignored (creating a new way of burning eth).
This would allow people to grief others at the cost of at least 512 ETH.

cc <@412614104222531604>

author: ben_a_adams
content: We have MAX_WITHDRAWAL_REQUESTS_PER_BLOCK and MAX_CONSOLIDATION_REQUESTS_PER_BLOCK; so it wouldn't be incongruous to add  MAX_DEPOSIT_REQUESTS_PER_BLOCK (i.e. 👍 is precedent)

author: mariusvanderwijden
content: <@316280621783580673> the max withdrawals and consolidations are limited by the system contract though, while the deposit contract does not have the limit. We don't limit it on the client side

If we'd go with only dequeuing the latest 512, then we'd need to keep track of logs of past blocks, which seems worse than the hardcap.

author: nero_eth
content: This number will change based on the repricings though. The max storage slots will be based on the gas limit in the cheapest way to get an item into the BAL - it should never be an additional constrain that is stricter than the gas limit.

author: nero_eth
content:
you always need to execute the tx for realizing.
E.g. tx0 does 512 deposits.
tx1 consumes 15M gas and then does 1 deposit.
To get to that deposit of tx1, you wasted 15M gas of "work". If every tx would come with a field saying how many deposits it includes then things would be different (as it would become static info) but that's not the case.

author: nero_eth
content: So the safest thing after adding tx0 to a block would be to stop adding any further txs, or at least stop doing so at some deadline.

author: chfast
content: How about a tx which uses 15M gas and then adds 513 deposits?

author: barnabasbusa
content:
afaik we have 3 options:
A change the deposit contract 
B add deposit limit on EL and basically not allow ELs to build a block with over X number of deposits (current eip proposal)
C mark CL blocks invalid that contain over x number of deposits. Kind of weird logic not even sure how this would work in practice. Block would need to reorged out and make it orphan in case we have a malicious client. 

I really don't think A is reasonable to ship in glamsterdam. 

Am I missing any other alternatives?

author: nero_eth
content: Same issue, even worse.

author: nero_eth
content:
What is the max nr of deposits one can do within 2**24 gas?

My feeling is we either change the deposit contract or have it resolved on the CL side.

author: nero_eth
content: Ah, I thought it was 512. That's just what you tested with?

author: barnabasbusa
content: 512 is just 2^9 and close enough to the 696 that we never even hit once

author: chfast
content: This is basically the DAO rescue soft-fork idea: nobody includes a transaction which withdraws from the DAO. It quickly turned out to be big DOS vector: you can ask a node/build to do a lot of work (e..g fetch big amount of state) and then do the forbidden action: your transaction cannot be included so you payed 0 for the work.

author: barnabasbusa
content: <@755590043632140352> one of your comments in ACD call was refering that "it can easily be solved on the CL side" did you mean we could invalidate CL blocks somehow easily if we see a deposit over x deposits in there?

author: nero_eth
content: Same issue then. You'd force tx simulation in the mempool for a tx that turns the block invalid

author: nero_eth
content: The CL would need another queue that comes with "no work", basically

author: potuz
content: I meant that the CL could ignore these payloads and make invalid any CL block that builds on them. This would be trivial to do on our side. The problem is that builders then need to be smart not to produce these blocks

author: potuz
content: but I think <@401469655669276673> already is hinting to the problem there

author: barnabasbusa
content: I think we can assume that builders wouldn't wanna be malicous. If a block with 512+ deposits would be reorged, the builder would be pissed

author: chfast
content: It's not a build who is malicious, but any transaction in the pool. You basically can flood the txpool with transactions having very high priority fee so builder will process them just to realize in the very end of transaction execution this transaction will make the whole block invalid. So it has to drop the transaction. Then it picks up the next one and the situation repeats. And the attack is for free because such transaction are not includable by spec so you never have to pay for them.

author: barnabasbusa
content: how do we limit max blobs per block?

author: barnabasbusa
content: ah i guess blob txs is a new tx type, so its easy to limit those

author: nero_eth
content:
It's basically this.
You can infer the nr of blobs of blob txs by just looking at them, it's static info. No execution/simulation needed. This is different for deposits.

author: nero_eth
content:
We'd need the tx to revert instead of invalidating the block to avoid a building DoS. For that the contract would need to change (or deposits need to be repriced significantly).
If that's not feasible (which seems like it's not) then the CL needs to solve that problem.
